title: 100 Years of Genius: The Toshiro Mifune Hall of Fame | The Big Picture
format: For Mifune's centenary and the show's 250th episode, Sean Fennessey and Chris Ryan build a Hall of Fame for Toshiro Mifune: the 16 films he made with Akira Kurosawa, sorted into an inner and outer ring, plus the work he did with everyone else.

segments.heading: Why Mifune
segments.note: the case for the greatest movie actor of his generation

films.title: Yojimbo
films.note: Chris's 101-level entry point: the menace and action fused with physical comedy.

films.title: Seven Samurai
films.note: His Kikuchiyo is nearly a Charlie Chaplin turn: expressive, glee-drunk, hilarious inside a movie no one remembers as funny.

films.title: I Live in Fear
films.note: At about 36 he plays a 65-year-old patriarch; it took Sean 45 minutes to realize the old man was Mifune.

films.title: High and Low
films.note: Designed for the first hour so he can dominate every room. Think Shaq in the late '90s: you just let him dunk.

segments.heading: The Toshiro Mifune Hall of Fame
segments.note: the Kurosawa collaborations, chronological, sorted into inner ring and outer ring

films.title: Drunken Angel
films.note: Where Kurosawa and Mifune first meet; the first post-war modern yakuza movie. Outer ring. First team-up with Takashi Shimura, who plays the sage doctor.

films.title: Stray Dog
films.note: Inner ring for Sean. A summer movie to set beside Do the Right Thing: a rookie cop loses his gun and it's off to the races. Invents the older-cop/younger-cop team-up.

films.title: Scandal
films.note: A solid film in the transition toward the morality plays.

films.title: Rashomon
films.note: Inner ring, and now a verb. Mifune studied caged lions for the bandit; Scorsese calls him a caged animal.

films.title: The Idiot
films.note: Their Dostoevsky adaptation, part of the early-'50s morality-play run.

films.title: The Lower Depths
films.note: Their Maxim Gorky adaptation; for Sean the most well-known, least successful of the collaborations.

films.title: Throne of Blood
films.note: Inner-ring top two. Functionally Macbeth in 16th-century Japan; the ending, Mifune shot at with real arrows, is Chris's favorite movie ending of all time.

films.title: The Hidden Fortress
films.note: Outer ring. The template George Lucas built Star Wars from: Obi-Wan, Leia, and Vader all traceable here.

films.title: Yojimbo
films.note: Inner ring. The most pop movie they made: a ronin plays two clans against each other. 'I'll get paid for killing and this town is full of men who deserve to die.'

films.title: Sanjuro
films.note: The Yojimbo follow-up; its final duel invents the arterial blood spray that Peckinpah and Kill Bill inherit.

films.title: I Live in Fear
films.note: Nuclear-dread drama; Mifune, aged way up, plays a patriarch trying to move his family to South America. Resonant on a 2020 rewatch.

films.title: The Bad Sleep Well
films.note: A Hamlet mashup and, per Sean, one of the most cynical movies he's seen, a Coen-brothers cold. Outer ring.

films.title: High and Low
films.note: Inner ring; based on an Ed McBain novel. A shoe executive must decide whether to ransom his chauffeur's kidnapped son. The height of the moral-dilemma film.

films.title: Red Beard
films.note: Their last film together, 16 in 18 years. Mifune plays a doctor and, for once, fights with his hands, not a sword: a closed loop back to Drunken Angel.

segments.heading: Beyond Kurosawa
segments.note: the soft five, the American work, and where each career went next

films.title: The Life of Oharu
films.note: A Mizoguchi film; Mifune lasts about 30 minutes as a noble warrior who sacrifices himself for love, a romantic register you don't see elsewhere.

films.title: Samurai I: Musashi Miyamoto
films.note: Hiroshi Inagaki's Samurai trilogy, shot in Technicolor. A huge influence on Kill Bill; Chris's real discovery of the rewatch.

films.title: The Sword of Doom
films.note: One of the better-known non-Kurosawa samurai films worth seeking out.

films.title: Samurai Rebellion
films.note: The other well-known non-Kurosawa samurai film Sean flags.

films.title: Grand Prix
films.note: John Frankenheimer's F1 movie; Mifune plays a Honda-founder figure who hires James Garner to race. Probably his best-known American work.

films.title: Hell in the Pacific
films.note: A near-wordless two-hander with Lee Marvin, directed by John Boorman before Deliverance. Pure face-and-body acting.

films.title: Red Sun
films.note: The one where he fights Charles Bronson; in the Criterion set, though Sean's not sure you need it.
Spielberg's famously maligned comedy, a lot of not-funny Belushi. Spielberg never made a comedy again.

films.title: Dersu Uzala
films.note: Kurosawa without Mifune, made in Russia after a decade in the wilderness and a suicide attempt. Won him an Oscar.

films.title: Kagemusha
films.note: Part of Kurosawa's late-career comeback run.

films.title: Ran
films.note: Kurosawa's late Shakespearean epic; recommended alongside the rest of the comeback.

films.title: Akira Kurosawa's Dreams
films.note: Kurosawa's late classic, again without Mifune.

films: The Treasure of the Sierra Madre

The Treasure of the Sierra Madre.title: The Treasure of the Sierra Madre
